channel.title: MiSTerZine
channel.description: A collection of quotes from around the web on MiSTer FPGA releases, each with its source linked.

item.title: Blockade (50th decadeversary)
item.description: Gremlin's 1976 arcade game Blockade "spawned many clones which came to be known as snake games" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Bubble Bobble (40th decadeversary)
item.description: Taito's 1986 arcade game Bubble Bobble was "one of the first games to feature multiple endings": beat all 100 levels by yourself, and it "reveals a message stating that the game has not truly ended", with the hint "Come here with your friend" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Joust 2 - Survival of the Fittest (40th decadeversary)
item.description: In Williams' 1986 arcade game Joust 2 - Survival of the Fittest, the player rides a flying ostrich, and the sequel adds "a transformation ability that morphs the player's bird into a pegasus, which provides better offensive capabilities while on ground but poor flight capabilities" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: The Return of Ishtar (40th decadeversary)
item.description: Namco's 1986 arcade game The Return of Ishtar, the sequel to The Tower of Druaga, is "an adventure game that requires two players", where "the screen will only scroll according to Ki's location, so the second player will have to stay close to their partner as they traverse the tower" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Die Hard Arcade (30th decadeversary)
item.description: Sega's 1996 arcade beat-em-up Die Hard Arcade is a Die Hard movie tie-in in the West, where players "play as either John McClane or Kris Thompsen". In Japan, though: "Because Sega did not hold the Japanese video game rights to the franchise, the game was stripped of the Die Hard license in Japan and published as an original property" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Atari Lynx (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: Atari's 1989 Lynx handheld connects units for multiplayer over a ComLynx cable, but that connection was nearly wireless: "The networking system was originally developed to run over infrared links and codenamed RedEye. This was changed to a cable-based networking system before the final release as the infrared beam was too easily interrupted when players walked through the beam, according to Peter Engelbrite" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Star Soldier: Vanishing Earth (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: Star Soldier: Vanishing Earth is Hudson Soft's 1998 shooter, part of the Star Soldier series. "The Star Force series, along with Hudson's Star Soldier, was often featured in the popular Japanese gaming tournament known as "Hudson All-Japan Caravan Festival". Home ports of Star Soldier games would sometimes feature "Caravan" modes in which the player would race through timed stages while trying to accumulate a high score." (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Salamander (40th decadeversary)
item.description: Konami's 1986 shooter Salamander was renamed Life Force in North America, where "the American version changes the game's plot by adding an opening text that establishes the game to be set inside a giant alien life-form which is infected by a strain of bacteria" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Tower & Shaft (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: Aruze's 2003 Tower & Shaft is "an arcade game based on both NS-Tower and NS-Shaft", two shareware Macintosh games from Nagi-P Software, "a Japanese software company founded in 1989 by Akihiko Kusanagi at the age of 13" (per HandWiki).

item.title: Eleven Beat (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: Eleven Beat, a Hudson Soft arcade game, ran on Seta's Aleck 64, a board that "essentially consists of a Nintendo 64 board retrofitted with sound capabilities that were standard for arcade games of the time." It was "designed by SETA Corporation in cooperation with Nintendo", yet "Nintendo did not release any arcade game using this hardware" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Tempest (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: Atari's 1981 arcade game Tempest is a tube shooter, and its designer, Dave Theurer, says the design "came from a nightmare where monsters crawled out of a hole in the ground". From there, he "basically just took Space Invaders and wrapped the surface into a circle" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Magical Tetris Challenge (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: Capcom's 1998 arcade game Magical Tetris Challenge is Tetris "featuring Disney characters". "The last three stages pit the player's character against a Weasel and the Big Bad Wolf before facing the game's main antagonist, Pete" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Arkanoid (40th decadeversary)
item.description: Taito's 1986 arcade block breaker: "Arkanoid was part of a contest within Taito, where two teams of designers had to implement a block breaker game and determine which was superior to the other" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Wonder Boy (40th decadeversary)
item.description: Sega's 1986 arcade platformer Wonder Boy was developed by Escape. "Escape had a licensing arrangement whereby it owned the rights to the game design, but Sega retained the Wonder Boy trademark." "To get around the licensing issue, Hudson Soft had the title changed which resulted into Adventure Island. Although Hudson could have featured the generic boy from Wonder Boy as the protagonist of Adventure Island, it instead created a brand new character, Master Higgins" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Rygar (40th decadeversary)
item.description: Rygar is Tecmo's 1986 arcade platformer, named for its hero. Per Wikipedia, "the Roman syllables "Ry" and "Li" both come from the same Japanese character, meaning there is no differentiation between "L" and "R" sounds. Due to this ambiguity, when the game was translated into English, the villain retained the name Ligar, but the hero was mistakenly called Rygar."

item.title: Rolling Thunder (40th decadeversary)
item.description: Namco's 1986 arcade game Rolling Thunder is a run-and-gun. Ryan Lambie, writing for Den of Geek, considers it "the precursor to the modern cover shooter" for the way "the player can hide behind crates, doors and other obstacles to avoid enemy fire" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: X-Men vs. Street Fighter (30th decadeversary)
item.description: Capcom's 1996 arcade game X-Men vs. Street Fighter is a tag-team fighter: each player picks two characters and switches between them mid-fight. Of the home port: "due to the memory limitations of the PlayStation ... the tag team combat was omitted" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Out Run (40th decadeversary)
item.description: Sega's 1986 arcade racer Out Run gave players "a radio with soundtrack selection". Per Wikipedia, "the game's selectable radio music has become commonplace in video games such as the Grand Theft Auto series".

item.title: Sea Wolf (50th decadeversary)
item.description: "In the United States, Sea Wolf was the highest-grossing arcade video game for two years in a row: 1976 and 1977." And: "Sea Wolf is one of the first video games to include a high score feature, saving the highest value achieved prior to powering off" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Renegade (40th decadeversary)
item.description: "Conceptualized and designed by Yoshihisa Kishimoto, the game was semi-autobiographical, partly based on his own teenage high school years getting into daily fights, with Kunio partly based on himself" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Fairchild Channel F (50th decadeversary)
item.description: Chipmaker Fairchild's Channel F, released in 1976, was the first console "to be based on a microprocessor and to use ROM cartridges" rather than games built into the box (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Atari Jaguar (MiSTer debut May 2026)
item.description: Console maker Atari released the Jaguar home console in 1993. "Atari tried to downplay competing consoles by proclaiming the Jaguar was the only "64-bit" system; in its marketing in the American market the company used the tagline "Do the math!", in reference to the 64 number. This claim was widely derided as misleading, because the Motorola 68000 CPU and the Tom and Jerry coprocessors execute 32-bit instruction sets." Electronic Gaming Monthly ran a mini-editorial: "If Sega did the math for the Sega Saturn the way Atari did the math for their 64-bit Jaguar system, the Sega Saturn would be a 112-bit monster of a machine" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Gulf War II (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: Gulf War II is a 1991 vertical arcade shooter by game maker Comad, a studio in Seoul, South Korea. It is not entirely Comad's own work: the game is "a very extensive hack of Twin Cobra", the 1987 coin-op by shooter studio Toaplan, "although Toaplan wasn't credited in-game". The game is titled Gulf War II, but "there was never a Gulf War I" (per BootlegGames Wiki).

item.title: Run and Gun (MiSTer debut July 2025)
item.description: Of the cabinet behind Konami's 1993 arcade game Run and Gun, known as Slam Dunk in Japan: "The cabinet is a unique design with dual monitors showing opposite ends of the court in proper perspective" (per the Museum of the Game).

item.title: Act-Fancer Cybernetick Hyper Weapon (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: Data East's 1989 arcade run-and-gun Act-Fancer has a lineage its title never advertises: "the goofily named Act-Fancer is actually a spinoff of the Darwin series", the maker's vertical shooters. Both share an evolution system: power up and the creature you control morphs into stronger forms, but the progress is fragile, since "a single hit will take you back to the weakest, almost useless form, and you still need to grab orbs to keep from devolving", writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

item.title: Hotdog Storm (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: In game maker Marble's 1996 vertical shooter Hotdog Storm, food appears only on the high score screen, "which features flying hot dogs and condiment bottles", writes SCROLL.

item.title: Mazinger Z (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: Banpresto's 1994 arcade game Mazinger Z is a vertical shooter based on the manga of the same name by Go Nagai, and it stayed a Japan-only coin-op until Hamster Corporation reissued it for the Arcade Archives line in 2023. "It was the first game in the series to be based on a non-game property; due to the involvement of Nagai himself, Toei Animation and Banpresto owner Bandai Namco Entertainment, Hamster sold the game at double the series' usual price to pay the license fees" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Rampage (40th decadeversary)
item.description: The three playable monsters in Bally Midway's 1986 arcade game Rampage began as people: "George, a scientist who transformed into a King Kong-like gorilla due to the influence of experimental vitamins; Lizzie, a woman who transformed into a Godzilla-like reptile after swimming in a lake contaminated with radioactive waste; and Ralph, who transformed into a gigantic werewolf after unwittingly eating a hot dog with an unknown food additive" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Star Wars (MiSTer debut May 2026)
item.description: Of one marathon on Atari's 1983 arcade game Star Wars: "In June 1985, Flavio Tozzi, Dave Roberts and Mike Ohren played as a team in turns for five days, two hours and 26 minutes on a single credit to attain the world record score of 1,000,000,012 points." "Their efforts raised money for a local charity" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Blood Bros. (MiSTer debut May 2026)
item.description: Blood Bros. is a 1990 arcade shooter by TAD Corporation, a Wild West follow-up to the studio's own Cabal. "Player one is a cowboy and player two is a Native American", and "the goofy factor has been raised significantly higher, with your most likely source of munitions of all things being pigs": "Red ones give you dynamite, black ones drop shotguns, and pink ones dispense the heavy machine gun", writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

item.title: Caveman Ninja (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: Data East's 1991 arcade platformer Caveman Ninja stars "the green-haired Joe and the blue-haired Mac, cavemen who battle through numerous prehistoric levels". Its Japanese version opens with "a beginning scene in which cavemen enter a hut and emerge while dragging cavewomen by their hair." That scene "was removed from the US release", and Data East explained the cut: "We didn't want kids to see [the Japanese display] and think it was okay" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Warrior Blade (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: Warrior Blade - Rastan Saga Episode III is Taito's 1991 arcade beat-em-up, the third Rastan game. Its player character, Rastan, fights with the help of a magical ally, "an ancient wizard named Mahadidekaradi", whose screen-clearing spells each come with "a lengthy text incantation that flashes on the screen as your enemies are destroyed." His foes take after the Conan stories of author Robert E. Howard: "the bosses appear to be unstoppable demons, but all crumble and die very anti-climatically, with just a single scream as they become lifeless and fall apart", writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

item.title: Legionnaire (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: Legionnaire is a 1992 arcade beat-em-up by TAD Corporation, the studio "mostly known for the gallery shooter Cabal". It "once had a reputation as a lost game": its copy protection "prevented it from being properly emulated in MAME", and so "it was basically forgotten until the MAME team decrypted it," writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

item.title: The Ninja Warriors (MiSTer debut May 2026)
item.description: The Ninja Warriors is Taito's 1987 arcade side-scroller. "Since the ninjas are technically robots, they take battle damage. If you take too many blows to the head, your mask and face peel away to reveal a cyborg skull similar to The Terminator," writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

item.title: Captain America and The Avengers (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: Data East's 1991 Marvel arcade beat-em-up sends the Avengers through occasional flight stages. Of the bosses there, writes Hardcore Gaming 101, "Amongst the most amusing of these is a fight against a robotic octopus called" Mech Taco. "Its goofy name obviously has nothing to do with the Mexican food, but rather that" tako "means" octopus "in Japanese, a rather hilarious language flub in a game featuring some overwhelmingly American action heroes".

item.title: Metamoqester (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: Metamoqester is Banpresto's 1995 arcade fighter. It was "originally released in Japan in November 1995" as the "pronounceable" Oni - The Ninja Master. Of the name it carries everywhere else, "why/how they came up with the name they did is still a mystery", writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

item.title: Apple IIGS (40th decadeversary)
item.description: The "GS" in the name stands for "Graphics and Sound". On the sound half of that: "The Apple IIGS's sound is provided by an Ensoniq 5503 DOC (Digital Oscillator Chip) wavetable synthesis chip designed by Bob Yannes, creator of the SID synthesizer chip used in the Commodore 64." Of that chip: "The inclusion of a professional-grade sound chip in the Apple IIGS was hailed by both developers and users, and hopes were high that it would be added to the Macintosh; however, it drew another lawsuit from Apple Corps." "As part of an earlier trademark dispute with the business arm of The Beatles, Apple Computer had agreed not to release music-related products" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Apple Lisa (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: Apple's 1983 Lisa "was the first mass-market personal computer operable through a graphical user interface (GUI)". Officially, Apple "stated that the name was an acronym for 'Local Integrated Software Architecture'". But "because Steve Jobs's first daughter was named Lisa (born in 1978), it was sometimes inferred that the name also had a personal association". Apple software developer Andy Hertzfeld said the acronym "was reverse-engineered from the name 'Lisa' in late 1982 by the Apple marketing team". "Privately, Hertzfeld and the other software developers used 'Lisa: Invented Stupid Acronym', a recursive backronym, and computer industry pundits coined the term 'Let's Invent Some Acronym' to fit Lisa's name" (per Wikipedia). Decades later, Jobs told his biographer Walter Isaacson: "Obviously it was named for my daughter."

item.title: Boogie Wings (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: Boogie Wings, Data East's 1992 arcade game, "is set around the time of World War I, where the player maneuvers biplanes, automobiles, animals, and various other unidentifiable objects to battle an army of mech-wielding scientists." Among them, "one of the game's bosses is a giant robot Santa Claus" (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Crude Buster (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: In Data East's 1990 brawler, released in the West as Two Crude, "players control Biff and Spike, two mercenaries hired by the American government to stop the terrorist organization Big Valley." "Because the duo are muscle-bound brawlers, they have the ability to pick up objects well beyond their own weight (e.g. cars and traffic lights) to use as weapons." Writing for Computer and Video Games magazine, Julian Rignall called Crude Buster "one of the best beat'em ups I've seen in years" and rated it 95% (per Wikipedia).

item.title: Air Gallet (30th decadeversary)
item.description: "When people talk about the fall of Toaplan, four companies always come up: Cave, Raizing, Takumi, and Gazelle," writes Hardcore Gaming 101. Gazelle made only a few games before fading into obscurity, among them Air Gallet, this 1996 vertical shooter published by Banpresto. Of one boss: "It's a large statue of a demon, surrounded in flames, choking others with its long, tentacle-like hair. It has some impressive attacks and starts crying blood once you blow off enough turrets."

item.title: Pretty Soldier Sailor Moon (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: "It is the first game to be created by Gazelle, one of the offshoots of defunct developer Toaplan that were founded after they declared bankruptcy in 1994," with "Knuckle Bash designer Junya Inoue serving as one of the game's graphic designers" (per Wikipedia). That May, Japan's Game Machine "listed Pretty Soldier Sailor Moon ... as being the eleventh most-successful arcade game of the month, outperforming titles such as Virtua Fighter."

item.title: Ikari Warriors (40th decadeversary)
item.description: "Amongst the numerous Commando clones on the market, Ikari Warriors was distinguished by its unique control style with rotary joysticks and a two-player cooperative mode," per Wikipedia. The game "was originally intended to be an official licensed adaptation of the film Rambo: First Blood Part II (1985), but SNK were initially unable to acquire the rights to the film." Even so, "Sylvester Stallone, whose role as John Rambo had an influence on the game, owned an Ikari Warriors arcade cabinet in the 1980s."

item.title: Fantasy Zone (40th decadeversary)
item.description: "The main character, Opa-Opa, is sometimes referred to as Sega's first mascot character," and the 1986 game, alongside Konami's TwinBee, is "credited with establishing the cute 'em up subgenre" (per Wikipedia). To arm the little ship, "the player must get money by defeating enemies, bases or bosses, and access a shop by touching a marked balloon."

item.title: Big Karnak (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: In Gaelco's 1991 coin-op, per Wikipedia, "players assume the role of a pharaoh warrior who embarks on a journey through four increasingly difficult levels taking place in an Ancient Egypt setting to save his wife by defeating the Egyptian gods Horus, Nut, Isis and Osiris." The studio "decided to work on a horizontal-scrolling arcade game similar to Double Dragon, Bad Dudes Vs. DragonNinja and Ghouls 'n Ghosts." It stayed an arcade exclusive for decades: "The title was never ported to other systems, until its inclusion as part of the Gaelco Arcade 2 compilation for Evercade."

item.title: Breakout (50th decadeversary)
item.description: "The reason Atari wanted me to design it is they were tired of their games taking 150, 200 chips, and they knew I designed things with very few chips, so we had incentives for getting it under 50 or under 40 chips," Steve Wozniak said of Breakout in a 2007 interview with Gamasutra. Atari's engineers, faced with the finished board: "They had trouble understanding it."

item.title: Night Slashers (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: "Data East also created Night Slashers, clearly their best beat-em-up and undoubtedly capable of cracking a connoisseur's Top 10 list, if it weren't for the game's unbelievable obscurity," reads a 2006 review at HonestGamers, which sums up the appeal: "watch the heads roll, the chests implode, and the guts soil the cement."

item.title: Trio The Punch (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: "Nothing about this game is done well, and yet everything just meshes together so perfectly into one gigantic dose of pure nonsense," writes Hardcore Gaming 101, which also says Trio The Punch "is like a visual representation of the writings of William S. Burroughs if he was a Japanese video game designer."

item.title: Battle Garegga (30th decadeversary)
item.description: "A number of shoot-em-up fans regard it as one of the best shooters of all time; others feel that the cluttered visual style and rank system ruin some pretty cool ideas," writes Hardcore Gaming 101. That rank system reacts to how well you play, and "creates some ridiculous scenarios where players will intentionally suicide at points to make the difficulty manageable, and can only properly clear it by scoring high enough to rack up extra lives."

item.title: Cabal (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: "The rather obscure Japanese TAD Corporation wondered what it would be like to give a third-person point of view to gun-based carnage," writes Hardcore Gaming 101, crediting Cabal with the wave of imitators that followed, Wild Guns and NAM-1975 among them: "None of these games would likely exist if Cabal didn't set the table."

item.title: Biomechanical Toy (MiSTer debut July 2026)
item.description: "The colorful, trippy graphics are reminiscent of many Amiga games," writes Hardcore Gaming 101 of Spanish maker Gaelco's run-and-gun. "It's not really on the level of other genre greats like Metal Slug (though it did predate that game by about a year)."

item.title: Flash Boy (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: Flash Boy shipped on Data East's DECO Cassette System, tape-loaded arcade hardware where "the machine was turned on and 2 to 3 minutes later the game was loaded and ready to be played," as AtariProtos describes it. "The tape medium didn't prove to be nearly as robust as Data East had hoped, and the cassettes had a bad habit of failing to load after only a few months."

item.title: Darius II (MiSTer debut June 2026)
item.description: "In addition to a three-screen release, there is also a (more common) two-screen version," notes Hardcore Gaming 101; the shot above is the full three-screen spread, while the MiSTer core runs the two-screen one. "For some unknown reason, Darius II was renamed Sagaia outside of Japan."
